title: The Nanny From Hell

jokes.text: A little plumbing. Got to plumb. Plumb the depths... the depths of hell.
jokes.setup: Larry explains he's doing plumbing work
jokes.punchline: Escalates from mundane plumbing to 'depths of hell' with dramatic delivery
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry - turns ordinary task into grandiose metaphor. Good character voice and escalation structure.

jokes.text: You ought to try schlepping marble one day. It's a schlep.
jokes.setup: Larry asks if someone has ever moved marble
jokes.punchline: Redundant observation delivered with conviction - marble schlepping is a schlep
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - uses 'schlep' twice in different contexts. Larry's tendency to state the obvious with authority.

jokes.text: Hey, Hugh... that sounds funny.
jokes.setup: Hugh introduces himself
jokes.punchline: Larry immediately comments on how the name sounds
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry social awkwardness - pointing out something everyone already knows but doesn't say.

jokes.text: He coined the phrase, 'The blank from hell.'
jokes.setup: Larry pitches Richard Lewis for Bartlett's Quotations
jokes.punchline: Claims Richard invented an obviously ubiquitous phrase
jokes.explanation: Great setup for the episode's theme. Larry's delusional confidence about Richard's 'contribution' to language is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Speedo. Speedo.
jokes.setup: Larry asks if Jeff is wearing a bathing suit to the pool party
jokes.punchline: Jeff's proud declaration he'll wear a Speedo (given his physique)
jokes.explanation: Visual comedy gold - Jeff's confidence about wearing revealing swimwear. Economy of delivery sells it.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, it sounds great. No, it is, it's really good. It's good, trust me. It'll all be fine.
jokes.setup: Jeff describes his reconciliation with pregnant Susie
jokes.punchline: Excessive, desperate reassurance reveals his obvious misery
jokes.explanation: Jeff's transparent overcompensation reveals his true feelings about reconciliation

jokes.text: I was saying to my wife, 'You should let me date, because it'll bring us much closer...'
jokes.setup: Jeff discusses marriage
jokes.punchline: Elaborate fantasy about how dating other women would improve his marriage
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic - Jeff's twisted rationalization is both ridiculous and somehow characteristic of his wishful thinking.

jokes.text: It's so moist, it's like eating a sponge.
jokes.setup: Defending sponge-cake quality
jokes.punchline: Description that makes it sound unappetizing while trying to sell it
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic sales pitch - 'like eating a sponge' as a positive selling point. Sets up the sponge-cake running gag beautifully.

jokes.text: No wonder you're going out of business.
jokes.setup: Bakery charges $12.95 for cake
jokes.punchline: Larry immediately connects high price to business failure
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry bluntness - saying what everyone thinks but wouldn't say. Perfect economy and timing.

jokes.text: 'It's like eating a delicious sponge.' Did I tell you?
jokes.setup: Jeffrey compliments the sponge-cake
jokes.punchline: Larry's smug vindication about his cake choice
jokes.explanation: Good callback structure. Larry's need to be right about trivial things is perfectly captured.

jokes.text: It's moot, they're going out of business. / That's not moot. / How's that not moot? / It's not moot.
jokes.setup: Jeffrey wants to know which bakery
jokes.punchline: Circular argument about whether the information is 'moot'
jokes.explanation: Great escalating wordplay. The repetition of 'moot' becomes increasingly absurd. Classic Curb argument structure.

jokes.text: You have to use the cabana. They won't let you in the house.
jokes.setup: Larry asks about the bathroom
jokes.punchline: Revelation about the ridiculous bathroom policy at the party
jokes.explanation: Sets up the central conflict. The absurdity of a bathroom hierarchy at a party is a perfect Curb premise.

jokes.text: I'd love to make a concession... An exception?
jokes.setup: Worker wants to help but can't break rules
jokes.punchline: Malapropism - 'concession' instead of 'exception'
jokes.explanation: Small but effective malapropism. Larry's correction shows his character's attention to language precision.

jokes.text: You're gonna go number one, right? Yes. I'll be done in two seconds. Just number one!
jokes.setup: Worker reluctantly allows bathroom access
jokes.punchline: Awkward insistence on specifying type of bathroom use
jokes.explanation: Great cringe moment. The specificity makes it funnier and more uncomfortable.

jokes.text: Nobody can turn down the David charm once I turn it on.
jokes.setup: Larry explains how he got into the bathroom
jokes.punchline: Grandiose self-assessment of his 'charm'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry delusion. The irony of calling it 'charm' when he clearly bulldozed his way in is quintessentially Larry.

jokes.text: It's bigger than mine. He's got a bigger penis than me.
jokes.setup: Larry and Jeff see Hugh's young son
jokes.punchline: Larry's inappropriate observation about child's anatomy
jokes.explanation: Peak cringe comedy. Larry's complete lack of social filter creates the central conflict for the episode.

jokes.text: It's like a freak show... We got a freak show here.
jokes.setup: Child's anatomy situation
jokes.punchline: Larry's insensitive carnival barker commentary
jokes.explanation: Larry doubling down on inappropriateness. Cheryl's discomfort amplifies the cringe factor.

jokes.text: It's like having a 'whisper lunch.' Like the Mafia thing.
jokes.setup: Cramped restaurant seating
jokes.punchline: Larry comparing their situation to organized crime secrecy
jokes.explanation: Creative metaphor combining restaurant complaint with mob movie reference. Good observational premise.

jokes.text: This is the lunch from hell. / Where'd you hear that expression?
jokes.setup: Richard complains about the restaurant
jokes.punchline: Larry catches Richard using 'his' phrase
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback execution. The irony of Richard unconsciously using his 'own' phrase is beautifully constructed.

jokes.text: You've got arms on your chair. Look at that. I got no arms, what is that?
jokes.setup: Business meeting about restaurant crisis
jokes.punchline: Larry fixating on chair inequality during serious discussion
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry priorities - petty concerns during crisis. His complete inability to read the room is perfectly Larry.

jokes.text: And for some reason I had a side dish of applesauce. I have to tell you it was really fantastic.
jokes.setup: Restaurant meeting about finding a chef
jokes.punchline: Larry's irrelevant anecdote about applesauce during crisis
jokes.explanation: Larry's complete disconnect from the situation's urgency. His tangential thinking is painfully characteristic.

jokes.text: You buy it at a supermarket. It comes in a jar.
jokes.setup: Larry suggesting applesauce for restaurant menu
jokes.punchline: Hugh's dry reality check about applesauce sourcing
jokes.explanation: Perfect straight-man response. Hugh's deadpan dismissal of Larry's 'brilliant' insight is economical and effective.

jokes.text: The kid's got some penis on him. He's pretty good.
jokes.setup: Larry talking to Hugh after the pool party
jokes.punchline: Larry bringing up the inappropriate observation again
jokes.explanation: Larry's inability to let things go creates the episode's major conflict. The doubling down is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: I'm not talking about your wife's tits. This is rude. / You could say my wife has nice tits as long as it's complimentary.
jokes.setup: Hugh objects to the penis comment
jokes.punchline: Larry's logic about equivalent inappropriate compliments
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of Larry's twisted logic. His attempt to justify inappropriate comments with more inappropriate examples is peak absurdist Larry.

jokes.text: Hugh, not 'you,' Hugh, okay? H-yoo... Hugh.
jokes.setup: Larry struggling with Hugh's name
jokes.punchline: Awkward pronunciation lesson during tense moment
jokes.explanation: Callback to earlier name observation. Larry's pedantic focus during conflict is perfectly timed cringe comedy.

jokes.text: It seems that one of the guests felt the cabana bathroom was beneath him and made his way into the main house.
jokes.setup: Cheryl explains nanny's firing to Larry
jokes.punchline: Describes Larry's bathroom breach in formal, accusatory language
jokes.explanation: Cheryl's diplomatic description highlights Larry's entitled behavior

jokes.text: No, I don't 'dime,' I didn't dime.
jokes.setup: Martine explaining she didn't tell on Larry
jokes.punchline: Awkward use of slang 'dime' (snitch)
jokes.explanation: Character-specific speech pattern. Martine's awkward slang usage hints at her eccentric nature.

jokes.text: Except I don't stink. Your home is big.
jokes.setup: Martine explaining her homelessness
jokes.punchline: Random observation about not smelling and Larry's house size
jokes.explanation: Establishes Martine's strange logic and non-sequitur speaking pattern that will escalate throughout the episode.

jokes.text: I happened to mention that I caught a glimpse of his son's penis... How about that thing? It's huge!
jokes.setup: Larry explains fight with Hugh to Cheryl
jokes.punchline: Repeats inappropriate comment with renewed enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Larry's inability to understand why comment was wrong, delivered with fresh excitement

jokes.text: Before that, I worked at Magic Mountain in the 'Looney Tunes' Lodge for 15 years.
jokes.setup: Asked about her work experience
jokes.punchline: Revealing bizarre previous job that explains her mental state
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character backstory that perfectly explains her later breakdown. The specificity of '15 years' makes it funnier and sadder.

jokes.text: She tells me she likes to pet horses. She enjoys a good corn dog.
jokes.setup: Larry describing Martine's strange conversation
jokes.punchline: Random biographical details that make no sense in context
jokes.explanation: Perfect example of Martine's non-sequitur personality. The mundane randomness is unsettling and funny.

jokes.text: I don't want the nanny from hell in my house. Where did you hear that... 'the blank from hell'?
jokes.setup: Cheryl uses common expression
jokes.punchline: Larry immediately questions phrase ownership again
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback showing Larry's continued delusion about phrase origin

jokes.text: She takes baths with her socks on.
jokes.setup: Continuing description of Martine's oddities
jokes.punchline: Deeply weird personal habit
jokes.explanation: Perfectly bizarre and specific detail. Creates vivid, uncomfortable mental image that defines Martine's strangeness.

jokes.text: My dad has been saying that... Richard Lewis coined that, Cheryl.
jokes.setup: Cheryl mentions her dad used the 'from hell' phrase
jokes.punchline: Larry insisting Richard Lewis invented common expression
jokes.explanation: Good callback that reinforces Larry's delusional loyalty to Richard's 'achievement' despite contradictory evidence.

jokes.text: This is a win-win situation. Win-win.
jokes.setup: Larry proposes giving problematic nanny to Jeff
jokes.punchline: Describes obviously terrible idea as 'win-win'
jokes.explanation: Larry's delusional optimism about passing his problem to friend

jokes.text: How many sponge-cakes do you have left? I have a dozen left. I'll take them all.
jokes.setup: Larry buying cake for Jeff's birthday
jokes.punchline: Buying entire remaining stock of closing bakery
jokes.explanation: Sets up the episode's climax perfectly. Larry's excessive gesture will have consequences, classic Curb structure.

jokes.text: Larry, he's not fat enough as it is? He needs this many?
jokes.setup: Susie sees Larry brought dozens of cakes
jokes.punchline: Blunt assessment of Jeff's weight and Larry's poor judgment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Susie - brutally honest about Jeff's weight while attacking Larry's logic. Character integration is flawless.

jokes.text: A big bird told me. / You knew I was pregnant? It's bad luck!
jokes.setup: Martine reveals knowledge of Susie's pregnancy
jokes.punchline: Susie's superstitious anger about pregnancy disclosure
jokes.explanation: Martine's odd expression and Susie's explosive reaction create good tension escalation.

jokes.text: You 'clapse'? Yeah, I'll collapse. You don't say 'co-llapse,' you say 'claps.'
jokes.setup: Richard threatens to collapse
jokes.punchline: Larry corrects pronunciation during crisis
jokes.explanation: Larry's compulsive word policing continues even during emotional moments

jokes.text: That's the 'porn baby'? Why is the porn baby here?
jokes.setup: Hugh's son appears at the screening
jokes.punchline: Richard's inappropriate nickname for the child
jokes.explanation: Dark callback that shows how Larry's observation has spread. 'Porn baby' is uncomfortably memorable.

jokes.text: You shush. Shush! / You shh, 'poopyhead.' / No, you shush, dodo brain.
jokes.setup: Child disrupting Richard's screening
jokes.punchline: Larry arguing at child's level with playground insults
jokes.explanation: Larry's regression to childish behavior is perfectly characteristic. The escalating insult exchange is well-structured.

jokes.text: Fuck h-yoo. / Fuck you. / Fuck Hugh! / Hugh!
jokes.setup: Final confrontation between Larry and Hugh
jokes.punchline: Name pronunciation confusion during profanity exchange
jokes.explanation: Perfect climactic callback. The name confusion creating an accidental pun during their fight is brilliant construction. Becomes the signature moment of the episode.

jokes.text: She worked at that 'Looney Tunes' Lodge for 15 years and she kept hearing that song over and over. She must have snapped from hearing it.
jokes.setup: Larry explaining Martine's breakdown
jokes.punchline: Revelation that cartoon theme song drove nanny insane
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark payoff to earlier setup. The idea that children's entertainment could create psychological damage is perfect Curb subversion.

jokes.text: My fall was broken by... 12 sponge-cakes.
jokes.setup: Susie explaining how she survived the nanny's attack
jokes.punchline: Larry's excessive cake purchase accidentally saves her life
jokes.explanation: Perfect climactic callback that ties together multiple storylines. Larry's seemingly pointless gesture becomes life-saving in the most absurd way possible. Classic Curb circular structure executed flawlessly.

tmdb_overview: Larry, Cheryl, Jeff and Susie encounter a ""nanny from hell"" whose reign of terror is thwarted by ten sponge cakes. Larry commits a ""penis faux pas"" after a pool party, and Richard Lewis seeks immortality from Bartlett's Quotations.
